coredata.dc:title: Understanding the energy level matching relationships between semiconductor photocatalysts and organic pollutants for effective photocatalytic degradations
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Tong, Haixia
Zhan, Xiaojun
Tian, Xiong
Li, Junhua
Qian, Dong
Wu, Daoxin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, representative semiconductors CdS, V2O5, WO3 and P25 (Degussa TiO2 consisting of 80 wt% anatase and 20 wt% rutile) were chosen as the photocatalysts, and the common phenol along with its derivatives (p-nitrophenol, p-chlorophenol and hydroquinone) were designated as the probe pollutants. The energy levels of the frontier molecular orbitals (E HOMO and E LUMO) of organics, and the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) energy levels (E VB and E CB) of photocatalysts were obtained through experiments and calculations. By correlating the photocatalytic activities of photocatalysts for the degradations of the organic pollutants under the irradiation of a 300 W xenon lamp with their above-mentioned energy levels, some new findings can be acquired. To be specific, the more positive E HOMO of an organic pollutant leads to its easier photodegradation by photocatalysts. Meanwhile, the more negative VB position for a photocatalyst results in the higher photocatalytic activities for the degradations of organic pollutants. Therefore, the matching correlation between the E HOMO of an organic pollutant and the E VB of a photocatalyst is of great importance to the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Phenol and its derivatives
Degradation
Energy level matching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparations of CdS, V2O5 and WO3
Fabrications of CdS-ITO, V2O5-ITO, WO3-ITO and P25-ITO membranes for the Motto-Scottky tests
Fabrication of GO/SWCNT–Nafion modified glassy carbon electrode
Fabrication of GO/SWCNTs–Nafion modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE)
Characterizations of photocatalysts, phenol and its derivatives

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( No. 21171174 and 11405013 ), Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Hunan (No. 09JJ3024 ), Hunan Provincial Education Department of outstanding youth fund (No. 0115003 ), Scientific Research Project of Housing and Urban Construction Department of Hunan Province ( KY201623 ), and Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project (No. 2016TP1007 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of P25, CdS, V2O5 and WO3.
TEM images of (a) CdS, (b) V2O5, (c) WO3 and (d) P25.
Motto-Scottky curves of (a) CdS, (b) V2O5, (c) WO3 and (d) P25.
UV–Vis DRS profiles of P25, WO3, CdS and V2O5.
CV curves of (a) phenol, (b) hydroquinone, (c) p-nitrophenol and (d) p-chlorophenol.

The photocatalytic degradations of phenol over various photocatalysts: (a) the dependence of the phenol concentration on the irradiation time, and (b) the kinetics properties of the photocatalytic degradation of phenol.
(a) The dependence of the phenol and its derivative concentration on the irradiation time, and (b) the kinetics properties of the photocatalytic degradations of phenol and its derivatives.
HOMO and LUMO orbital positions of (1) phenol, (2) hydroquinone, (3) p-nitrophenol and (4) p-chlorophenol, and the VB and CB energy levels of P25.
LUMO and HOMO orbital positions of phenol, and energy levels of CdS, V2O5, P25 and WO3.
E f, E VB, E g and E CB of the semiconductor photocatalysts.
E O, E R, E HOMO and E LUMO of phenol and its derivatives calculated from the CV curves.
Degradation rate constants of phenol over various photocatalysts.
Degradation rate constants of phenol and its derivatives over P25.
ΔE values between the VB energy levels of catalysts and the HOMO orbital position of phenol calculated from Fig. 9.

row.entry: E VB of catalyst (eV, vs. vacuum)
E HOMO of phenol (eV, vs. vacuum)

head.ce:title: Understanding the energy level matching relationships between semiconductor photocatalysts and organic pollutants for effective photocatalytic degradations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410114, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical and Biological Engineering
Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Metal-Organic Compounds of Hunan Province, College of Chemistry and Material Science, Hengyang Normal University, Hengyang 421008, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Metal-Organic Compounds of Hunan Province
College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, representative semiconductors CdS, V2O5, WO3 and P25 (Degussa TiO2 consisting of 80 wt% anatase and 20 wt% rutile) were chosen as the photocatalysts, and the common phenol along with its derivatives (p-nitrophenol, p-chlorophenol and hydroquinone) were designated as the probe pollutants. The energy levels of the frontier molecular orbitals (E HOMO and E LUMO) of organics, and the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) energy levels (E VB and E CB) of photocatalysts were obtained through experiments and calculations. By correlating the photocatalytic activities of photocatalysts for the degradations of the organic pollutants under the irradiation of a 300 W xenon lamp with their above-mentioned energy levels, some new findings can be acquired. To be specific, the more positive E HOMO of an organic pollutant leads to its easier photodegradation by photocatalysts. Meanwhile, the more negative VB position for a photocatalyst results in the higher photocatalytic activities for the degradations of organic pollutants. Therefore, the matching correlation between the E HOMO of an organic pollutant and the E VB of a photocatalyst is of great importance to the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation.

ce:section.ce:para:
Since Carey’s group [1], for the first time in 1976, employed TiO2 to degrade polychlorinated biphenyls under visible light irradiation, photocatalytic degradations of organic pollutants have been attracting intensive interest of researchers. Considering the current energy and environmental issues on a global scale, semiconductor photocatalysis is a pressing demand for industrial applications. However, due to the low efficiency of photocatalytic degradations of organic pollutants, their widespread industrial applications have not realized so far. For semiconductor photocatalysts, researchers ascribe the main bottleneck to the low separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers including electrons (e−) and holes (h+), which usually results in low photo-degradation efficiency. In general, to achieve the high performance of a photocatalyst, the high redox potentials of photo-generated charge carriers, i.e. high oxidation power of h+ in the valence band (VB) and enough reduction power of e− in the conduction band (CB), are essential prerequisites [2]. So far, a wide range of semiconductors, such as TiO2 [3–9], ZnO [10–13], SnO2 [14,15], ZrO2 [16], CdS [17,18], Cu2O [19,20], Bi2O3 [21], BiVO4 [22,23], SrTiO3 [24,25], and Fe2O3 [26–28] have been investigated to degrade different organics including phenol, rhodamine B, methylene blue, methyl orange and so on by photocatalysis. Previous studies have indicated that the photocatalytic performances are different for either the same catalyst to degrade different organic pollutants or different catalysts to degrade the same organic pollutant. To get more insight into the photocatalysis, considerable efforts have focused on the band structures of photocatalysts [28,29]. Some researchers have noticed that when the band structures of the semiconductors meet the requirements of redox potentials of the organics, the photo-generated e− and h+, hydroxyl radicals (
OH), and superoxide anion radicals (
O2−) play important roles in the photocatalytic oxidation processes [30–37], especially
OH [37]. However, little attention has been paid to the energy matching between the organic pollutants and the photocatalysts [38].

From the theory of oxidation–reduction, the complicated relationships among the h+ potentials (E VB, namely, the VB energy level) of photocatalysts, the e− potentials (E CB, namely, the CB energy level) of photocatalysts, and the formation potentials of different free radicals often lead to distrinct photocatalytic degradation mechanisms. On the other hand, as some organic contaminants such as dyes can also be photoexcited to produce e− and h+, there should exist correlations between the energy levels of the frontier molecular orbitals (E HOMO and E LUMO) of organic contaminants and E VB and/or E CB of photocatalysts in the photocatalytic degradations of organic contaminants. In this regard, Shi et al. [38] calculated the related E HOMO and E LUMO of organic contaminants employing a Gaussian 03 program, and found that the E LUMO played a vital role in the photocatalytic degradations of methylene blue, Rhodamine B and Rhodamine 640 by g-C3N4/BiOCl. Specifically, the more positive E LUMO of an organic contaminant results in its easier photodegradation by photocatalysts. However, to our best knowledge, no works have been documented on the matching correlations between the E HOMO of organic contaminants and E VB of photocatalysts. To this end, representative semiconductors CdS, V2O5, WO3 and P25 (Degussa TiO2 consisting of 80 wt% anatase and 20 wt% rutile) were chosen as the prototype photocatalysts, and the common phenol along with its derivatives (p-nitrophenol, p-chlorophenol and hydroquinone) were adopted as the probe pollutants to investigate their energy level matching relationships for effective photocatalytic degradations.

Phenol and its derivatives were purchased from Jinan Hengtai Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Benzoquinone was gained from Guangzhou Liyuan Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Tertbutyl alcohol was received from Shandong Beiduobei Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). NH4VO3 and ammonium wolframate were bounght from Tianjin Guangfu Institute of Fine Chemicals (China). P25 was supplied by Shandong Degussa Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Indium tin oxide (ITO) was purchased from Guangzhou Laibang Hi-Tech Co., Ltd. (China). Graphene oxide (GO) and single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) were obtained from Suzhou Tanfeng Graphene Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Na2S and CdCl2 were obtained from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Ammonium oxalate ((NH4)2C2O4) and AgNO3 were attained from Zhengzhou Zhiyi Chemical Products Co., Ltd. (China). All reagents were analytical grade and utilized as received without further purifications. Deionized water was used throughout all the experiments, and the CEL-HXF 300 W xenon lamp was purchased from Beijing CEAULIGHT Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations of CdS, V2O5 and WO3

ce:section.ce:para: CdS nanoparticles were prepared similarly to Zhou et al.’s report [39] except for the replacement of the stabilizer sodium hexametaphosphate with ethanol. Typically, 50 mL CdCl2 solution (0.1 mg L−1) and 20 mL ethanol were added into a triangular bottle, and 50 mL Na2S solution (0.1 mg L−1) was then added dropwise under stirring for 30 min. The precipitates were filtered, washed with respective deionized water and absolute ethanol several times, dried at 80 °C for 2 h, and subsequently ground into fine powders. Eventually, the powders were calcinated at 500 °C for 1 h in air at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 followed by ground for 30 min to give CdS nanoparticles.

10 g NH4VO3 was dissolved into 17% hot ammonia solution, and then dried at 80 °C for 4 h. After ground for 30 min, the obtained white powders were calcinated at 500 °C for 1 h in air at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1. Ultimately, V2O5 nanoparticles were obtained after ground for 30 min.
The preparation of WO3 was similar to that of V2O5 except for the replacement of NH4VO3 with ammonium wolframate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrications of CdS-ITO, V2O5-ITO, WO3-ITO and P25-ITO membranes for the Motto-Scottky tests
ce:section.ce:para: The ITO substrates were washed with detergent, acetone, isopropyl alcohol and deionized water under sonication in turn, succeeded by drying at 80 °C for 1 h in vacuum. 5 g P25 was mixed with polyethylene glycol 20,000 with a mass ratio of 100:1 with the addition of an appropriate amount of deionized water. The obtained mixture was ground for 30 min to form a slurry in an agate mortar. The resulting slurry was painted on the surface of an ITO substrate with the conducting side facing down. The P25-ITO membrane was received after the painted ITO substrate was calcined at 300 °C for 1 h in air at a ramp of 2 °C min−1. The CdS-ITO, V2O5-ITO and WO3-ITO membranes were fabricated as the same as the P25-ITO membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GO/SWCNT–Nafion modified glassy carbon electrode

ce:section.ce:para: 30 mg SWCNTs were added into a round bottom flask containing 15 mL of 0.2 mol L−1 HNO3 solution. The gained suspension was kept refluxing for 7 h under stirring. After naturally cooled down, SWCNTs were collected by centrifugation. Then the obtained SWCNTs were mixed with 10 mL of 36% HCl solution followed by sonication for 1 h. The acid-treated SWCNTs attained by centrifugation were washed with deionized water until the pH of water was neutral and dried at 100 °C for 10 h in vacuum to yield the purified SWCNTs. 6 mg purified SWCNTs and 6 mg GO were placed into 24 mL Nafion anhydrous ethanol solution (0.1%) succeeded by sonication for 2 h to achieve the GO/SWCNTs-Nafion dispersion polymer composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GO/SWCNTs–Nafion modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE)
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the modification, the GCE was polished with 0.3 and 0.05 μM alumina slurries, respectively. And then, the GCE was rinsed ultrasonically with ethanol for 10 min followed by drying in the N2 stream. After that, 5 μL GO/SWCNTs–Nafion composite was dropped onto the surface of GCE and dried in an infrared oven at 60 °C for 1 h. The operation was repeated twice, and the electrode was kept at 4 °C for 24 h in a refrigerator. The electrode was subsequently placed in ethanol, and the GO/SWCNTs-Nafion composite was scattered on GCE under sonication for 30 min. The GO/SWCNTs–Nafion modified GCE was fabricated eventually.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of photocatalysts, phenol and its derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: The phase structures of photocatalysts were identified by X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a HATCHI D/max 2250 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (0.154056 nm). The morphologies and particle sizes were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2011).

The analyses of Motto–Scottky curves were carried out to obtain the flat band potentials (E FB, vs. Ag/AgCl) of semiconductor photocatalysts. The Motto–Sottky curves were measured on a Gamry Reference 600TM Potentiostat/Galvanostat/ZRA electrochemical workstation in a three-electrode system, utilizing a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, a platinum wire electrode as the counter electrode, a photocatalyst-ITO membrane electrode as the working electrode, and 0.2 mol L−1 Na2S solution as the buffer. The testing frequency, system temperature, step length of scanning voltage and sine signal amplitude were 1 kHz, 1.4 °C, 100 mV and 10 mV, respectively.

UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was recorded to detect UV–Vis light absorption properties of photocatalysts on a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer with BaSO4 as the reference. The band gaps of photocatalysts can be estimated by the formula E g = 1240/λ g, where E g is the band gap energy (eV) and λ g refers to the maximum absorption wavelength (nm).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis was performed to detect and obtain the redox potentials of phenol and its derivatives on a Shanghai Chenhua electrochemical workstation applying a three-electrode system at room temperature, in which an Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode, a platinum wire electrode as the counter electrode, a GO/SWCNTs–Nafion modified GCE as the working electrode, and a phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution with pH = 7.4 as the buffer. The concentrations of phenol and its derivatives were all 100 mg L−1.

The photocatalytic tests were conducted in a 250 mL homemade optical reactor. A 300 W xenon lamp was placed about 5 cm away from the reactor (the intensity of the light employed was 100 mW cm−2). Typically, in the photocatalytic degradation of phenol, 10 mg photocatalyst (P25, CdS, V2O5 or WO3) was suspended in 50 mL phenol solution (20 mg L−1) under magnetic stirring in dark for 1 h to ensure a complete absorption/desorption equilibration of the organic pollutant on the photocatalyst. The requisite aliquot (1 mL) was withdrawn periodically, diluted by deionized water to 25 mL, and centrifuged to remove the residual catalyst powders. The concentration of phenol was detected by the 4-amino antipyrine spectrophotometry. The degradability of phenol was defined by C 0/C, where C 0 and C represented the molar concentrations of organic pollutant solution after stirring in dark and irradiating for a certain time, respectively. The photocatalytic degradation test for 50 mL p-nitrophenol (20 mg L−1), p-chlorophenol (20 mg L−1) or hydroquinone (20 mg L−1) by P25 (10 mg) was similar to the above photocatalytic degradation of phenol. The concentration of p-nitrophenol, p-chlorophenol or hydroquinone aqueous solution was detected by the UV spectrophotometry.

The XRD patterns of P25, CdS, V2O5 and WO3 are shown in Fig. 1 , and the characteristic peaks for these photocatalysts can be distinctly observed. It can be seen that some peaks are overlapped, which is caused by line-broadening resulting from the small crystal sizes of these oxides. Apparently, P25 consists of anatase phase and rutile phase. Fig. 2 presents TEM images of these four photocatalysts. It can be found that CdS appears aggregates composed of nanoparticles with sizes of 20–70 nm. V2O5 presents aggregates consisted of nanoparticles with sizes of ca. 20–60 nm. WO3 shows aggregates comprising nanoparticles with sizes of 20–50 nm. P25 is composed of nanoparticles with sizes of 20–50 nm.

The Motto-Scottky curves of CdS, V2O5, WO3 and P25 are illustrated in Fig. 3 . Obviously, all the slopes of four curves are positive on the whole, suggesting that CdS, V2O5, WO3 and P25 are all n-type semiconductors [40]. The distance from the origin to the point where the tangent of the curve intersects the horizontal axis reflects the E FB of the semiconductor. Accordingly, the obtained E FB values of CdS, V2O5, WO3 and P25 are −0.62, 0.39, −0.28 and 0.17 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively. When the position of reference electrode in the electrolyte is constant, the position of semiconductor energy level varies with the change of external voltage. When the applied external voltage is negative to a certain degree, the curved energy band of the semiconductor will be flattened out, and the applied external voltage is the E FB of the semiconductor [40]. Moreover, the E FB of a semiconductor denotes the difference between the Fermi energy level (E f) of the semiconductor and the potential of the reference electrode Ag/AgCl. The standard electrode potential of Ag/AgCl is 0.24 V at room temperature, and consequently the E f values of CdS, V2O5, WO3 and P25 are −0.38, 0.63, −0.04 and 0.41 V, respectively.

The UV–Vis DRS curves of CdS, V2O5, WO3 and P25 are given in Fig. 4 . As revealed from Fig. 4, apart from TiO2, the other three semiconductors hold responses to the visible light, and the maximum absorption wavelengths of P25, WO3, CdS and V2O5 are at around 400, 490, 550 and 580 nm, respectively. According to the formula E g = 1240/λ g, the E g values of P25, WO3, CdS and V2O5 are 3.1, 2.53, 2.25 and 2.14 eV, respectively.

Generally, E CB of an n-type semiconductor is about 0.2 eV higher than E f. On the basis of the following Eqs. (1)–(4) [31,41], the E f, E VB, E g and E CB of P25, WO3, CdS and V2O5 can be obtained, which are listed in Table 1 .
(1)
E CB
vs . NHE
= X - 0.5
E g
vs . NHE
+
E f
(2)
E VB
vs . NHE
=
E CB
vs . NHE
+
E f
(3)
E CB
vs . vacuum
=
E f
-
E CB
vs . NHE
(4)
E VB
vs . vacuum
=
E f
-
E VB
vs . NHE
where E g is the band gap, and X is the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor (geometric mean of the absolute electronegativity of the constituent atoms); E f is defined as the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (−4.5 eV), and it is converted to E f = −4.5 eV between hydrogen electrode potential and vacuum level potential; E CB and E VB are the CB and VB potentials of the photocatalyst, respectively.

Fig. 5 exhibits CV curves of phenol and its derivatives, from which the relative redox potentials can be derived. However, the reduction potential of p-nitrophenol cannot be acquired because the nitro group reduction of p-nitrophenol is a process involving proton participation. Of note, the reduction process of nitro group fails to occur in our test, due to the pH value of 7.4 for the employed solution [42]. Zhang et al. [43] achieved the reduction potential of 0.56 V for p-nitrophenol by using carbon encapsulated FeCu4 alloy nanoparticles modified GCE in the test solution with the pH value of 7.0. Hence, this value is adopted in this work. The potential of Ag/AgCl reference electrode is 0.2224 V (E θ Ag/AgCl = 0.2224 V) at 25 °C, and the E HOMO and E LUMO can be calculated by the following Eqs. (5)–(7) [44]:
(5)
0 eV
vs . vacuum
= - 4.5 V
vs . NHE
(6)
E HOMO
vs . vacuum
= - 4.5 -
E O
vs .
NHE
(7)
E LUMO
vs . vacuum
= - 4.5 -
E R
vs . NHE
where E O and E R signify the oxidation and reduction potentials of phenol and its derivatives, respectively. The received E O, E R, E HOMO and E LUMO are displayed in Table 2 .

From Table 2, it can be found that the HOMO and LUMO orbital positions (vs. vacuum) of hydroquinone are higher than those of phenol, while those of p-nitrophenol and p-chlorophenol are both lower. Thus, it can be concluded that the electron-withdrawing groups (i.e. –NO2 and –Cl) make the energy levels of LUMO and HOMO lower, and the electron-donating group (i.e. –OH) prompts the orbital positions of LUMO and HOMO higher, which coincide with the previous reports [39,43].

Fig. 6 shows the degradation performances of phenol under the xenon light irradiation over P25, WO3, V2O5 and CdS. As portrayed from Fig. 6(a), the degradation rate of phenol over P25 is the highest among these photocatalysts, which reaches 90% after the irradiation for 480 min.

The degradations of phenol over these four photocatalysts can be well fitted by the first order kinetics (Fig. 6(b)), and the corresponding degradation rate constants (k) of phenol are calculated by the following Eq. (8), which are listed in Table 3 .
(8)
ln
C /
C 0
= - k t

From Table 3, it can be realized that the activities of the photocatalysts follow the order: TiO2 > WO3 > V2O5 > CdS. Interestingly, it can be found that there exists a correlation between the activity of a photocatalyst and E VB as disclosed in Table 1. Specifically, the more negative E VB (vs. vacuum) of a photocatalyst brings about higher photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 7 illustrates the degradations of phenol and its derivatives under the xenon light irradiation over P25. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the degradation rate of hydroquinone over P25 is the highest among these organics, which achieves 100% after 240 min irradiation. As evidenced by Fig. 7(b), the degradations of phenol and its derivatives also adhere to the first order kinetics, and the order of the corresponding degradation rate constants (k) is hydroquinone > phenol > p-nitrophenol > p-chlorophenol, which are presented in Table 4 .

To correlate the E HOMO and E LUMO of phenol and its derivatives with the E VB and E CB of P25, the corresponding orbital positions and energy levels are drawn in Fig. 8 . As seen from Fig. 8, the electron-withdrawing groups (i.e. –NO2 and –Cl) which lower the LUMO and HOMO positions of phenol decrease the degradation efficiency, and the electron-donating group (i.e. –OH) which elevates the LUMO and HOMO positions of phenol enhances the degradation efficiency. Therefore, the higher HOMO orbital position of an organic pollutant leads to its easier photodegradation by photocatalysts, which is also supported by the previous experimental results [45]. As examples, the phenol derivatives with electron-donating groups connected to the phenol ring can be more easily photodegraded by photocatalysts, whereas the phenol derivatives with electron-withdrawing groups connected to the phenol ring can be more difficultly photodegraded.

The LUMO and HOMO orbital positions of phenol, and the energy levels of CdS, V2O5, P25 and WO3 are depicted in Fig. 9 . From Fig. 9, the calculated differences (ΔE) between the VB energy levels of catalysts and the HOMO orbital position of phenol are listed in Table 5 .

As shown in Table 5, the order of ΔE between the VB energy levels of catalysts and the HOMO orbital position of phenol is CdS < V2O5 < WO3 < P25. According to the thermodynamics, when the Gibbs function ΔG < 0, the chemical reaction towards the positive direction occurs spontaneously. When the reaction system is in a state of constant temperature and standard pressure, there exists the following thermodynamics formula:
(9)
Δ r
G
m
θ
= - RT Ln
K
θ

where
Δ r
G
m
θ
represents the standard Gibbs function difference, R refers to the constant of ideal substance, T signifies the Kelvin temperature, and K θ stands for the standard reaction equilibrium constant. On the other hand,
Δ r
G
m
θ
equals −zFΔE as follows:
(10)
Δ r
G
m
θ
= - zF Δ E

where z corresponds to the migration number of electrons, F is the Faraday constant, and ΔE stands for the potential difference of primary battery. From formulae (9) and (10), the following derivation can be acquired:
(11)
zF Δ E = RT Ln
K
θ
⇒ Δ E =
RT Ln
K
θ
zF

It is not difficult to find that when the other reaction conditions are identical, the larger ΔE value results in the larger K θ value and therefore the more complete reaction proceeding towards the positive. Hence, from formula (11), it is easy to draw a conclusion that the order of the possibility for the photocatalytic reactions is P25 > WO3 > V2O5 > CdS, which is consistent with the above experimental results shown in Table 3. The low negative VB energy level is expected to endow h+ with a high oxidizing ability. As a result, h+ is easier to react with H2O or OH− for the formation of active
OH radicals. Thus, it is the low negative VB position that leads to the high photocatalysis performance of P25. Although the VB positions are almost the same in P25 and WO3, the higher positive CB position of P25 leads to higher oxidizing ability. Hence, the degradation of phenol over P25 is higher than WO3.

The HOMO and LUMO orbital energy levels E HOMO and E LUMO of phenol and its derivatives (p-nitrophenol, p-chlorophenol and hydroquinone) were obtained by the cyclic voltammetry tests, and the VB and CB energy levels of CdS, V2O5, WO3 and P25 are acquired by the Motto–Scottky tests and UV–Vis DRS spectra. By investigating the photocatalytic degradations of the selected pollutants by the above semiconductors under the irradiation of a 300 W xenon lamp, it can be found that the more positive E HOMO of an organic pollutant leads to its easier photodegradation by photocatalysts. Meanwhile, the more negative VB position for a photocatalyst results in the higher photocatalytic activities for the degradations of organic pollutants. Undoubtedly, the matching correlation between the HOMO orbital position of an organic pollutant and the VB energy level of a photocatalyst will pave a new avenue for the rational designs of high-performance photocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21171174 and 11405013), Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Hunan (No. 09JJ3024), Hunan Provincial Education Department of outstanding youth fund (No. 0115003), Scientific Research Project of Housing and Urban Construction Department of Hunan Province (KY201623), and Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project (No. 2016TP1007).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J.H. Chen, Z.Q. Gong, Ion doping of titanium dioxide semiconductor photocatalyst, Sci. Pre., Beijing, 2006, pp. 15−40 (Ch. 2).
